title: Amazon VP Reveals Everything He's Seen In Corporate Politics | Ethan Evans
guest_role: Former Amazon VP, executive career coach

sections.title: Handling difficult people from other orgs
Leverage when people are getting fired
How to grow past senior eng

key_takeaways: Empire building persists because headcount is the only promotion metric nobody can debate; after Evans left Amazon, some orgs required ~80-90 reports for director, effectively a several-hundred-thousand-dollar-a-year bonus for accumulating 90 people, and leaders parked people under candidates to check the box.

Reorgs are 'hidden chess': the public business narrative coexists with private motives like retaining key people with juicier scope, giving disfavored people less, or platforming someone for promotion; leaders construct defensible storytelling for auditing parties.

Quiet, dutiful high performers get unconsciously deferred for resources because management assumes they'll accept less; Evans tells coaching clients that being 'a very nice person' is not a compliment in this context — you must state what you want.

Express career urgency without threats: Evans told his manager 'I need to understand how important my career is to Amazon because my career is very important to me' — the same underlying message as a quit threat but without triggering the extortion defense; delivery, warmth and timing determine how identical words land.

Demanding high performers are easier to manage than nice ones: refuse them and they self-select out; instead make explicit deals (deliver on my priorities and I'll deliver your promotion) and always follow through — e.g. Evans got a senior manager to director by having him build Amazon's India dev center over three years.

Large companies run 'forward looking slates' of promotions over 6-24 month windows; modern quota systems (e.g. Google allowing two L8-to-L9 promos per half per org) create literal queues, and million-dollar senior IC positions are not handed out 'like popcorn'.

Never mutiny alone against a bad manager: a lone complaint is cheaper for the skip to dismiss than to believe (believing creates three problems: managing out, backfilling, absorbing the work); two or three corroborating reports flip the calculus.

To escape a bad manager, first try a pure business case for the move without mentioning the manager; failing that, keep it blame-free ('maybe it's him, maybe it's me, I need a change'); Evans unlocked his own blocked move overnight by reframing the choice as 'keep me on your team or lose me entirely' backed by a competing SVP offer.

Defending org scope from raids: bundle unglamorous essential work into the story, respond quickly and thornily to attacks, use strong metrics to delay, and 'be the better storyteller' — attackers underestimate others' complexity (S3 dismissed as 'just a big disk drive') because respect between teams is inversely proportional to distance.

Influence and politics are the same skill set distinguished only by motive ('Darth Vader had executive presence in spades'); treat cross-functional partners as people with competing pressures rather than APIs, use 'how can you help me' framing, and pre-plan opening moves like chess.

notable_quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
notable_quotes.quote: There's like a several hundred thousand dollars a year bonus quite literally for accumulating 90 people.

notable_quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
notable_quotes.quote: I will often tell my clients, you are a very nice person. And when I tell them that, what they don't know is I'm then going to say, and that is not a compliment in this context.

notable_quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
notable_quotes.quote: If you want to hold a mutiny, go together. Right? Even if it's Zelda, it's dangerous to go alone. Take this, take other people.

notable_quotes.speaker: Ethan Evans
notable_quotes.quote: Respect between two engineering teams is inversely proportional to their distance.
